analysis:
- Common strong structure: Pareto prefilter → rect‑volume (single‑point HV) surrogate → adaptive shortlist → stamped lazy‑greedy heap that recomputes exact marginal HV only when a candidate reaches the top → small bounded single‑swap refinement. This balances quality vs cost well.
- Good practices observed: using pygmo for exact HV when available, caching HV results, seeding heap by cheap surrogates, limiting outsider pools in swaps, adaptive shortlist sizes proportional to k.
- Recurrent overheads and inefficiencies:
  - Many implementations use Python O(N^2) loops for nondominated filtering and per‑candidate checks; this dominates time for large N.
  - Recomputing full HVs for many candidate unions is expensive; some heuristics call hv_of_set_rel redundantly or build heavy cache keys (frozenset/tuples) repeatedly.
  - Monte‑Carlo fallbacks vary: some build global shared samples (good), others perform fresh sampling per call (costly and inconsistent).
  - Use of Python sets/frozensets as cache keys causes allocation and hashing overhead. Numerous small Python loops (per candidate, per swap) reduce throughput.
  - Diversity seeding (farthest‑first) improves spread but can be costly if distances computed naively.
- Strong vs weak contrast:
  - Strong heuristics (1–7) carefully combine surrogates, lazy evaluation, and cached exact evaluations; they also tune shortlist sizes, use stamping to avoid recompute, and perform bounded local search.
  - Weaker heuristics (8–11) lean heavily on raw Monte‑Carlo coverage or naively recompute marginals without effective caching or lazy rules, which leads to either poorer hypervolume estimates or much higher runtime for similar quality.
- Concrete opportunities to reduce cost without quality loss:
  - Replace repeated full HV recomputations for MC approximations with bitset/boolean arrays of precomputed dominated samples; marginal gains become cheap vectorized OR/count operations.
  - Use a single global MC sample (or a small stratified set) and precompute per‑candidate coverage once; reuse for lazy greedy and swap tests.
  - Reduce Python-level loops by using vectorized numpy operations or compiled routines for nondominated sorting and distance/diversity calculations.
  - Limit exact HV (pygmo) calls to a tiny set: only the top few heap candidates per selection and only post‑selection in the swap refinement.
  - Make cache keys lightweight (sorted tuple of ints once, or a bitmask/index into interned frozenset) and avoid recreating keys inside inner loops.
experience: Favor a lazy‑greedy driven by cheap, precomputed Monte‑Carlo bitsets for fast marginal estimates and only call exact HV for the top ranked candidates. Shortlist adaptively (c·k), use vectorized nondominated filtering and bitset OR/count operations to evaluate gains, and keep caching lightweight. This yields near‑exact selections with minimal expensive HV calls and far lower Python overhead.
current_reflection: Favor a lazy‑greedy driven by cheap, precomputed Monte‑Carlo bitsets for fast marginal estimates and only call exact HV for the top ranked candidates. Shortlist adaptively (c·k), use vectorized nondominated filtering and bitset OR/count operations to evaluate gains, and keep caching lightweight. This yields near‑exact selections with minimal expensive HV calls and far lower Python overhead.
good_reflections: Always prefilter (non‑dominated), compute cheap single‑point estimates, drive a lazy max‑heap of marginal gains with stamps, recompute exact marginal HV only on heap top, then apply a small bounded local swap. Cache results, avoid rebuilding arrays/objects in loops, vectorize dominated checks, and cap stochastic search effort. This combo maximizes HV per evaluation at low runtime.

Prefer Pareto prefilter + stamped lazy greedy with single‑rect warmstarts and an adaptive shortlist (c·k). Cache HVs keyed cheaply (sorted tuple or bitmask), compute marginal = hv(S∪{p})−hv(S) only for heap tops, use pygmo when available, fallback to adaptive Monte‑Carlo with bitset chunking, and limit swap trials. Vectorize nondomination and avoid repeated frozenset/array conversions.

Keep the stamped lazy-greedy backbone: Pareto prefilter → adaptive rect‑surrogate shortlist → stamped heap + HV-cache → tiny bounded swap. Map original indices into a compact pool, compute HVs with pygmo when available, otherwise reuse a small shared MC sample for marginal estimates. Limit pairwise/diversity costs and cap shortlist/swap budgets adaptively by k to maximize HV per compute.

Seed a stamped lazy-greedy heap with normalized singleton rect scores, keep a compact shortlist ∝ k, cache HVs using canonical sorted tuples or bitmasks, and recompute exact marginal only for popped items. Reuse Monte‑Carlo dominated masks (bitsets) to compute marginals incrementally; adapt sample size to D and pool size. Limit and prioritize swap trials. Profile hotspots and avoid O(N^2) Python loops—vectorize or chunk.

Prefilter by Pareto, build an adaptive shortlist using a blended surrogate (rect volume + normalized shared global MC dominated counts + a lightweight diversity term), then run a stamped lazy greedy heap that recomputes true gains only at the top (use pgm exact HV when available). Cache bitset dominated masks and HV results, use a tiny focused swap pool, vectorize nondomination, and adapt sample/pool sizes to N, D, k for speed and reliable marginal estimates.
bad_reflections: Prefer Pareto prefilter + rect surrogate shortlist + stamped lazy heap, but enforce: (1) cached HVs keyed by frozenset(indices), (2) reuse a single pygmo.hypervolume object per distinct point set when possible, (3) vectorize dominance and sample precomputation, (4) adapt shortlist size to k and N, and (5) bound local swaps — this yields high HV with predictable runtime.

Always Pareto‑filter first, then shortlist by single‑point rect volumes plus a lightweight diversity pick. Use a stamped lazy greedy heap with exact HV from a fast C library; cache marginal gains keyed by compact bitmasks. Limit Monte‑Carlo to large‑D fallback with small M. Cap swap neighborhood and parallelize exact evaluations. Vectorize dominance checks and avoid hot Python loops or frequent frozenset creation.

Prefer stamped lazy‑greedy seeded by cheap surrogates, reuse one shared MC sample and dominated mask, cache HVs with lightweight keys, vectorize nondomination, adapt shortlist and M to k and D, and limit local search to top outsiders. These yield large time savings with little HV loss.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: stamped lazy‑greedy, Pareto prefilter, rect/MC surrogates, bitset dominated masks, adaptive shortlist (c·k), HV cache (bitmask/sorted tuple), pygmo fallback, bounded local swap.
- Advice: Prefilter Pareto; seed a stamped lazy heap with cheap rect/MC singleton scores; keep an adaptive shortlist ∝ k; compute marginals via vectorized bitset OR/count and call exact HV only for popped tops; cache HVs with lightweight keys; reuse shared MC samples; limit swap trials and shortlist size.
- Avoid: rebuilding arrays/objects in loops, O(N^2) Python loops, frozenset‑heavy keys, excessive exact HV calls or large swap budgets.
- Explanation: Near‑exact selections with minimal expensive HV evaluations and low Python overhead; adapt MC/sample sizes to D and pool for robustness.
